questions.question: What is the best first move when an interviewer gives incomplete sizing inputs?
options: Choose a plausible base case, bound it with a range, and state the assumption clearly.
Stop until exact production data is provided.
Pretend the missing number does not matter.
Use the highest possible number for every variable.
questions.explanation: Strong candidates keep moving while making assumptions explicit.

questions.question: Why are low/base/high estimates useful?
options: They show uncertainty honestly and help reveal which assumptions would change the architecture.
They guarantee the final answer is exact.
They eliminate the need for trade-off discussion.
They matter only for storage problems.
questions.explanation: Ranges are a decision tool, not just a hedge.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of identifying an architectural breakpoint under uncertainty?
options: You show when the current design stops being appropriate and what would need to change next.
You prove the current design is perfect at every scale.
You avoid estimating any cost numbers.
You remove the need for observability.
questions.explanation: Threshold thinking is the senior move under incomplete data.

questions.question: Why is varying every uncertain input at once usually a bad interview move?
options: It makes the discussion hard to reason about and hides which factor matters most.
It automatically overestimates storage only.
It guarantees the cheapest design wins.
It is fine because complexity always impresses interviewers.
questions.explanation: Too many simultaneous ranges reduce clarity instead of increasing rigor.

questions.question: Which follow-up best demonstrates mature uncertainty handling?
options: Explain which assumption has the highest leverage and how you would validate it later.
Refuse to continue until analytics data is exact.
Quote a single number and never revisit it.
Use worst-case values for every dimension and stop there.
questions.explanation: Sensitivity plus validation planning is the right combination.

questions.question: Which observability signal best checks whether your range assumptions were too optimistic?
options: Actual workload values approaching or exceeding the predicted breakpoint
The number of architecture slides in a review deck
The count of product managers on the project
The total number of source files
questions.explanation: You want to know when real behavior is leaving the planned envelope.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with choosing the base case as the initial optimization target?
options: You move faster and stay pragmatic, but you must clearly name how the design changes at the high end.
You guarantee the high case will need no redesign.
You minimize every possible cost automatically.
You eliminate the need to prioritize requirements.
questions.explanation: Base-case optimization is reasonable only if breakpoints are explicit.

questions.question: When is a simpler v1 architecture especially defensible under uncertainty?
options: When the low or base case fits comfortably and the threshold for a more complex design is still distant and observable.
When multi-region complexity is always mandatory.
When the interviewer has not asked any questions yet.
When exact costs are unknown.
questions.explanation: A simple start is strong when the future trigger for change is visible and manageable.
